The first season of the American [science fiction](/wiki/Science_fiction "Science fiction") television series *[Fringe](/wiki/Fringe_(TV_series) "Fringe (TV series)")* commenced airing on the [Fox network](/wiki/Fox_Broadcasting_Company "Fox Broadcasting Company") on September 9, 2008, and concluded on May 12, 2009. It was produced by [Bad Robot](/wiki/Bad_Robot "Bad Robot") in association with [Warner Bros. Television](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Television "Warner Bros. Television"), and its [showrunners](/wiki/Showrunner "Showrunner") were [Jeff Pinkner](/wiki/Jeff_Pinkner "Jeff Pinkner") and [J. H. Wyman](/wiki/J._H._Wyman "J. H. Wyman"). The first season introduces a [Federal Bureau of Investigation](/wiki/Federal_Bureau_of_Investigation "Federal Bureau of Investigation") "Fringe Division" team based in [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston"), [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts") under the supervision of [Homeland Security](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Homeland_Security "United States Department of Homeland Security"). The team uses unorthodox ["fringe" science](/wiki/Fringe_science "Fringe science") and FBI investigative techniques to investigate a series of unexplained, often ghastly occurrences, which are related to mysteries surrounding a [parallel universe](/wiki/Parallel_universe_(fiction) "Parallel universe (fiction)"). FBI agent [Olivia Dunham](/wiki/Olivia_Dunham "Olivia Dunham") is portrayed by actress [Anna Torv](/wiki/Anna_Torv "Anna Torv"), while actors [Joshua Jackson](/wiki/Joshua_Jackson "Joshua Jackson") and [John Noble](/wiki/John_Noble "John Noble") play father-son duo [Peter](/wiki/Peter_Bishop "Peter Bishop") and [Walter Bishop](/wiki/Walter_Bishop_(Fringe) "Walter Bishop (Fringe)"). Other regular cast members include [Lance Reddick](/wiki/Lance_Reddick "Lance Reddick"), [Jasika Nicole](/wiki/Jasika_Nicole "Jasika Nicole"), [Blair Brown](/wiki/Blair_Brown "Blair Brown"), [Mark Valley](/wiki/Mark_Valley "Mark Valley"), and [Kirk Acevedo](/wiki/Kirk_Acevedo "Kirk Acevedo").

The season produced 21 episodes, although only 20 of them aired as part of the first season. The unaired episode, "[Unearthed](/wiki/Unearthed_(Fringe) "Unearthed (Fringe)")", was broadcast as part of the second season as episode 11 as a special episode.

## Season summary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fringe_season_1&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Season summary")]

The first season of *Fringe* begins with the introduction of the main characters, as Olivia is brought aboard the Fringe division following the critical injury of her partner, John Scott, in an event tied to the Fringe division. As part of her investigation, she finds she needs the knowledge and experience of Walter Bishop, currently institutionalized in a mental hospital. Olivia blackmails Walter's estranged son, Peter, to be Walter's legal guardian, allowing his release from the institution. Olivia finds that Walter's knowledge in the area of fringe science to be critical for her job, and convinces Peter to remain as Walter's guardian; Peter, initially resentful due to events in his childhood, starts to participate directly in the cases, his abilities as a jack-of-all-trades being a benefit to both Walter's scientific needs and Olivia's investigations. Walter himself struggles with life outside the institution, plagued by a secret from his past and often resorting to recreational drugs. Olivia comes to discover that Massive Dynamic, a company that Walter used to be a part of, is connected with several of the Fringe cases.

The Fringe team starts to learn of a bio-terrorist group known as ZFT (in German, Zerstörung durch Fortschritte der Technologie, translated as "Destruction by Advancement of Technology"), which seem to be after several of Walter's old experiments. In one case, a group of men led by Mitchell Loeb have recreated Walter's technology used to pass through solid matter to steal several components of a device, constructed by Walter, from banks. With the assembled device, Loeb's team is able to teleport David Robert Jones, a former Massive Dynamics employee and leader of the ZFT, out of a high security prison. Jones attempts to sway Olivia to his side as a "soldier" in an upcoming "war," making her aware that she, as a child, was part of a test run by Walter and his partner William Bell of the [nootropic](/wiki/Nootropic "Nootropic") drug, Cortexiphan, that gave her psychokinetic powers. Olivia refuses, disbelieving his claim but realizing that evidence to support it appears true. Meanwhile, the Observer meets with Walter and takes him to Walter's old beach house, where he finds a device he once made that can close a portal to a parallel universe. Tracking Jones, Olivia finds that all of the Fringe events have formed a spiral, centering on Reiden Lake in New York, and she, Peter, and Walter are able to prevent Jones from crossing over to a [parallel universe](/wiki/Parallel_universe_(fiction) "Parallel universe (fiction)") using the device that Walter found, killing Jones in the process. Later Walter is seen at a cemetery where he mourns over a grave labeled "Peter Bishop."

Olivia tries to get answers from Nina Sharp on Jones' motive, but she only directs her to a meeting with Bell. Olivia, while en route to the meeting, finds herself in the parallel universe, in the [World Trade Center](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_(1973%E2%80%932001) "World Trade Center (1973–2001)") office of William Bell.

The woman was pregnant for a few minutes, yet the baby she birthed was fully developed - then aged eighty years in the span of another few minutes. They discover that the killer is an artificially aged human, who is using the hormones extracted from the removed glands to halt his [accelerated aging disease](/wiki/Accelerated_aging_disease "Accelerated aging disease"). Among the attacks is a collapse of the [Birmingham Bridge](/wiki/Birmingham_Bridge "Birmingham Bridge") in [Pittsburgh](/wiki/Pittsburgh "Pittsburgh"). Dr. Bishop hides the cylinder against the wishes of the rest of the team, owing a debt to the Observer for saving his life and that of his son years earlier. An unknown man abducts and tortures Peter for information regarding the cylinder's location, and learns its placement through the ideas Peter has absorbed from his father through [osmosis](/wiki/Osmosis "Osmosis"). The man takes him to a cemetery housing a tombstone bearing the name of Robert Bishop and finds the cylinder. Olivia shows up after learning the location from Walter and kills the man. The object burrows back underground before either side can discover its purpose. With the help of carrier pigeons modified to track the man's electromagnetic signature, the team manages to track him down and arrest the scientist. ## Development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fringe_season_1&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Development")]

> While we make sure that our episodes are self-contained – have a beginning, a middle, and an end – the character stories can be serialized. They don't have to resolve themselves over the course of one show.

–Co-creator Alex Kurtzman[[23]](#cite_note-buddytvcreators-25)

Co-creators [J. J. Abrams](/wiki/J._J._Abrams "J. J. Abrams"), [Alex Kurtzman](/wiki/Alex_Kurtzman "Alex Kurtzman"), and [Roberto Orci](/wiki/Roberto_Orci "Roberto Orci") began the brainstorming process for *Fringe* in the spring of 2008. They avoided emulating Abrams' other show, *[Lost](/wiki/Lost_(2004_TV_series) "Lost (2004 TV series)"),* because they believed its mysteries made it difficult to attract new viewers.[[24]](#cite_note-ew2009-26) Instead, the co-creators studied crime procedurals, such as the American series *[CSI: Crime Scene Investigation](/wiki/CSI:_Crime_Scene_Investigation "CSI: Crime Scene Investigation")* and the franchise *[Law & Order](/wiki/Law_%26_Order_(franchise) "Law & Order (franchise)"),* in an attempt to merge that genre with a more mythology-based show without creating only standalone episodes. Orci explained, "We studied procedurals specifically to try and merge the two. Very against our instincts to do that, but when nine of the top TV shows are called *Law & Order* and *CSI,* you have to study them a little bit and figure out what it is that they're doing."[[23]](#cite_note-buddytvcreators-25) Abrams has also listed the science fiction television series *[The Twilight Zone](/wiki/The_Twilight_Zone "The Twilight Zone")*, *[Altered States](/wiki/Altered_States "Altered States")*, and *[The X-Files](/wiki/The_X-Files "The X-Files")* as well as the 1979 film *[Alien](/wiki/Alien_(film) "Alien (film)")* as inspirations for *Fringe*.[[24]](#cite_note-ew2009-26)

[Jeff Pinkner](/wiki/Jeff_Pinkner "Jeff Pinkner"), an executive producer on two of Abrams' other television series *Lost* and *[Alias](/wiki/Alias_(TV_series) "Alias (TV series)")*, was hired to serve as [showrunner](/wiki/Showrunner "Showrunner") and executive producer for *Fringe.*[[25]](#cite_note-27) He described *Fringe* as being concerned with "primal fear", as "our bodies and our minds are, at the end of the day, unknowable. The ways our bodies betray us can be terrifying."[[26]](#cite_note-chicagotribuneryan-28)

Critics as well as those involved in the show's production have acknowledged that *Fringe* had a rocky beginning. Abrams later noted, "It's going to sound weird, but a show starts talking to you and telling you what it wants to be. It took us a while to hear it."[[24]](#cite_note-ew2009-26) The series struggled early on, as the first ten episodes had continuous rewrites and expensive last-minute reshoots. Actor [Lance Reddick](/wiki/Lance_Reddick "Lance Reddick") noted "It was a constant struggle. It just didn't feel like a signature [J.J. Abrams] show — not yet."[[24]](#cite_note-ew2009-26) Pinkner has also acknowledged that early episodes were often too neatly wrapped and solved. "We found that, absolutely, early on, we were falling into the trap of—the tease would be fantastic. And then we would too quickly answer it and [reduce] the tension," he said. "And we've tried to course-correct and have the tease promise" questions that don't get answered right away.[[26]](#cite_note-chicagotribuneryan-28)

The writers began to focus more attention on the series' mythology when Akiva Goldsman temporarily joined *Fringe* to write and direct a mid-season episode, "[Bad Dreams](/wiki/Bad_Dreams_(Fringe) "Bad Dreams (Fringe)")." Believing "this is the stuff fans want to know", he and Bryan Burk were able to convince the other series writers to begin revealing the concept of parallel universes, despite the writers' initial intention to merely tease the idea of two worlds over two seasons. They also attempted to make Olivia's personal life more relatable by ending her storyline with John Scott and giving her a sister and niece.[[24]](#cite_note-ew2009-26) Abrams noted that Olivia was evolving from a "guarded, protective woman" who did not have strong relationships with others to someone more vulnerable; the presence of Olivia's sister, he noted, helped give the character "at least opportunities to be warmer to someone."[[27]](#cite_note-29) By the middle of the season, critics[[28]](#cite_note-ignseasonreview-30) and regular viewers noted *Fringe* had started to improve.[[24]](#cite_note-ew2009-26) Kevin Reilly observed around this time, "I was looking for evidence that it was becoming their favorite show. And it was during the back half of the season that people started to say, 'This is now appointment television for me."[[24]](#cite_note-ew2009-26)

## Reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fringe_season_1&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Reception")]

### Ratings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fringe_season_1&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Ratings")]

*Fringe* finished its first season with an average of 8.8 million viewers per episode, making it the most watched new series for the 18–49 demographic.[[24]](#cite_note-ew2009-26) In Canada on [CTV](/wiki/CTV_Television_Network "CTV Television Network"), *Fringe*'s first season had an episode average of 1.3 million viewers and finished in 19th place.[[29]](#cite_note-31)

### Reviews

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fringe_season_1&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Reviews")]

On [Rotten Tomatoes](/wiki/Rotten_Tomatoes "Rotten Tomatoes"), the season has an approval rating of 84% with an average score of 7.5 out of 10 based on 25 reviews. The website's critical consensus reads, "Action-packed, suspenseful, and filled with intriguing twists, *Fringe* is a smart sci-fi series that's compelling enough to overcome its occasionally uneven plotting."[[30]](#cite_note-32) [Metacritic](/wiki/Metacritic "Metacritic"), a review aggregate website, gave the first season 67/100 based upon 25 critical reviews, indicating a "generally favorable" reception.[[31]](#cite_note-33) While the series was perceived to have a shaky start, the season finale aired to general fan acclaim.[[24]](#cite_note-ew2009-26)

## Home video releases

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fringe_season_1&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Home video releases")]

The first season of *Fringe* was released on DVD and Blu-ray in region 1 on September 8, 2009,[[32]](#cite_note-34) in region 2 on September 28, 2009[[33]](#cite_note-35) and in region 4 on September 30, 2009.[[34]](#cite_note-36) The sets includes all 20 episodes of season one on a 7-disc DVD set and a 5-disc Blu-ray set presented in anamorphic widescreen. Special features on the sets include three commentary tracks—"Pilot" with co-creators [J. J. Abrams](/wiki/J._J._Abrams "J. J. Abrams"), [Alex Kurtzman](/wiki/Alex_Kurtzman "Alex Kurtzman") and [Roberto Orci](/wiki/Roberto_Orci "Roberto Orci"), "The Ghost Network" with writers [J. R. Orci](/wiki/J._R._Orci "J. R. Orci"), [David H. Goodman](/wiki/David_H._Goodman "David H. Goodman") and executive producer [Bryan Burk](/wiki/Bryan_Burk "Bryan Burk"), and "Bad Dreams" with writer/director [Akiva Goldsman](/wiki/Akiva_Goldsman "Akiva Goldsman") and executive producer [Jeff Pinkner](/wiki/Jeff_Pinkner "Jeff Pinkner"). Episodic behind-the-scene featurettes include "Deciphering the Scene" on every episode and "The Massive Undertaking" on select episodes. Also included on select episodes are "Dissected Files", deleted scenes. The main featurettes include "Evolution: The Genesis of *Fringe*", "The Casting of *Fringe*", "The Real Science Behind *Fringe*" and "*Fringe* Visual Effects". Also included is "Roberto Orci's Production Diary", a short featurette titled "Gene the Cow" and a gag reel. Exclusive to the Blu-ray version is "*Fringe* Pattern Analysis".[[35]](#cite_note-37)
